preamble: The shift from the open-mid back unrounded vowel [ʌ] to the close front rounded vowel [y] is a rare but well-documented phenomenon in Celtic languages, where it occurs as part of the process of 'attenuation' or 'slenderization'. This involves a dramatic fronting and rounding of back vowels when followed by palatalized consonants.

examples.note: The broad vowel [ʌ] (orthographic 'u') shifts to the slender front rounded vowel [y] (orthographic 'ui') when the following consonant is palatalized (slenderized) in the genitive case or plural.
Example of 'u' [ʌ] alternating with 'ui' [y] in morphological paradigms.
Rounding and backness harmony: In suffix vowels, the mid-back unrounded [ʌ] (realization of /ɯ/) alternates with the front rounded [y] to match the features of the root vowel.
